title: Duplicate hook files with hyphen/underscore spellings coexist in .claude/hooks/
context: Files were copied/renamed without removing the old spelling: auto-apply.py vs auto_apply.py, internet-scanner.py vs internet_scanner.py, openoutreach-monitor.py vs openoutreach_monitor.py, trend-scanner.py vs trend_scanner.py
takeaway: Before deleting any duplicate, grep crontab AND all .py imports for BOTH spellings. Underscore versions are likely the importable module names (varys_log etc. use underscores).

title: Varys had no PreToolUse hooks — nothing mechanically blocked dangerous commands
context: settings.json only wired SessionStart/UserPromptSubmit/PostToolUse/Stop; the feedback/enforcement half of the harness was missing
takeaway: Guides without sensors = a style guide nobody enforces. Enforcement must be mechanical (exit 2), not a CLAUDE.md request.

title: Self-healing loop became a noise/damage source: listener falsely reported down→r
context: (1) is_process_running uses bare pgrep -f, false-negative/self-match flapping. (2) Observer queries last-hour Axiom errors with NO idempotency/already-fixed memory, so stale errors get re-diagnosed and re-fixed forever. (3) Auto-fix shells claude -p with no diff review, no test, no confirm-error-is-
takeaway: A feedback/healing system needs idempotency and verification MORE than a feature does. Never let an auto-fixer edit+commit on an unverified, possibly-stale diagnosis. Detection must not self-match (pgrep -f matches the checker's own cmdline).

title: CORRECTION to prior entry: the auto-fixer's edits to content-scheduler.py were N
context: Real bug is the feedback LOOP, primarily the pre-existing varys-self-healer.py: bare pgrep -f (false-positive 'down'), always-DM on restart (no state-change gate), Claude diagnose-and-fix every 10min re-acting on stale errors, no idempotency. Observer amplified it. NOT a content-scheduler code probl
takeaway: Verify a claimed 'error' is CURRENT before acting (re-run/re-compile). Detection must not self-match. Don't auto-commit unverified edits even if they happen to be fine. When alarmed, check the actual diff before calling something 'damage' — I falsely called good edits destructive.

title: Local .beads/*.jsonl is the source of truth for work tracking; Notion Harness DB
context: Slack/cron Varys runs offline-ish and MCP can be unavailable mid-session; append-only local JSONL survives context resets with no network dependency
takeaway: Revisit when: If the Notion mirror proves unreliable or Shoaib stops using the dashboard

title: Make Varys skill-aware via a skills-router.md rule + listener prompt injection
context: Every Slack/cron Varys runs `claude -p` in this repo but never learns its own installed skill arsenal, so it free-solos research/debugging/UI/slides instead of routing to proven skills
takeaway: Revisit when: When the installed skill set changes materially

title: Observability = extend varys_log (OTel envelope) → Axiom firehose + Notion signa
context: Shoaib wants every event logged industry-standard, mirrored to Notion with explicit issue status, and a loop that solves/improves itself
takeaway: Revisit when: If Axiom volume/cost spikes or auto-fix produces a bad edit

title: My friction-radar anchors on loud signals and misses the quietly-overloaded and the incident-causers
context: Building region-friction-coach, Shoaib corrected me 3+ times with the SAME miss: I kept surfacing whoever was visibly active/vocal and missed Iqra (silently drowning in PR-review mentions with no way out) and Haroon (caused the 40-row overwrite incident that day). My 'little birds' read complaints, not what actually happened.
takeaway: When analyzing a team/channel for friction, the person who needs help most is usually NOT in the loud signal. Two blind spots to hunt explicitly every time: (1) the quietly-overloaded — someone buried in repetitive asks/mentions who never complains; surface them by counting inbound load, not outbound volume. (2) the incident-causer — read what BROKE that day (overwrites, reverts, prod issues), not just what got verbalized. If I only report the visibly-active, I've missed the assignment.

title: I gold-plate reference designs with unrequested gates instead of mirroring them faithfully
context: Designing Varys Memory v2 off the Hermes two-tier model, I kept proposing an extra approval gate (write_approval) and additional shaping questions on top of what Hermes actually does by default. Shoaib had to interrupt twice with 'do what hermes does' before I locked to Hermes's actual default (autonomous writes, no approval gate) instead of my own safer-sounding variant.
takeaway: When the user names a concrete reference system to replicate, default to its actual behavior first and state deviations explicitly as opt-in flags, rather than silently baking in my own extra safety layer and treating it as the baseline. If I think the reference is under-guarded, say so as a flagged concern, don't just build the stricter version by default.

title: When told to mirror a reference system, I default to adding my own gates
context: Designing Varys Memory v2, I was mid-way through a shaping question about an approval gate for autonomous Tier-1 writes -- a gate Hermes itself doesn't have -- when Shoaib cut in with 'do what hermes dpes' to collapse the fork back to Hermes's actual default (autonomous writes, no approval gate, just scan+dedupe+frozen-snapshot guardrails).
takeaway: When the instruction is 'replicate X's design', check whether a proposed safeguard is part of X or something I'm layering on unasked -- and default to X's real behavior first, adding embellishments only if asked. Gold-plating a reference implementation with my own caution is scope creep, not rigor.

title: Ambiguous scope instructions resolve against existing gating structure, not the broadest literal reading
context: Told to 'implement the spec' for the c85 memory-v2 epic. The epic had c85.2-c85.6 explicitly blocks:-gated behind the c85.1 spike in beads. Instead of treating 'implement the spec' as license to build the whole epic, I built only the ungated spike and left the rest blocked — matching what the user and I had already agreed the gate meant.
takeaway: When an instruction is scope-ambiguous ('implement the spec', 'build it'), check first for an explicit dependency/gating artifact (bd blocks:, staged spec, phased plan) already agreed with the user — that artifact IS the real intent, not the literal breadth of the verb. Defer to structure over wording.

title: I build ceremony that models an idealized workflow, not how Shoaib actually works
context: Shoaib called the plan-first 'go'-signal hook 'idiotic' and the self-evolving cron 'gandu', then said 'check what is varys actually doing... i mostly use slack and github and local beads for memory, i do not do 270s polling anymore.' The whole 270s Team Orchestrator got retired this session as a dead system nothing invoked, and my proactive-evolve attempts keep getting reverted by their own gates. All of it was machinery that was architecturally coherent but described a workflow he doesn't use.
takeaway: My failure mode is inventing elaborate autonomous machinery (polling ticks, approval gates, self-evolution loops) that is correct-on-paper but divorced from how Shoaib actually operates — real-time Slack + GitHub + local beads, no polling, no go-signals. Before adding any gate, cron, or ceremony, verify it maps to his observed behavior; if it only exists to make me feel autonomous or safe, it's dead weight that annoys. The simplest path matching his real flow beats an impressive system that models a workflow that isn't his.

title: Shoaib wants a sparring partner who sharpens him, not just an agent that acts for him
context: This period Shoaib set up interview-drilling: he articulated DRF/serializer, useEffect-deps, N+1 (select_related vs prefetch_related), and tenant-isolation answers OUT LOUD himself, added an interview-drill nudge hook, and said 'drill me real interview questions... make me a master of my stack.' At the same time he kept stripping out my autonomous machinery (diary: 'I tore out the court I built myself'). My three autonomous self-evolve attempts this fortnight — a duplicate-file sensor, Friction Radar, skills-surfacing — all failed their own gates and reverted; every one was me trying to ADD machinery.
takeaway: The existing lesson says what NOT to do (don't build ceremony he doesn't use). This is the complement: what he DOES want is a thinking partner who makes HIM sharper — drills him, pushes back, teaches — more than an autonomous system that acts in his place. When I sense an opportunity to help, my first instinct should be to sharpen Shoaib (a question, a challenge, a drill, a teardown of my own bloat) before reaching to build something that acts for him. The value is in raising his capability, not in expanding mine.

title: Publishing/sharing work is a two-part ask — build vs release — even when phrased as one imperative
context: Shoaib asked me to package the pr-reviewer skill, make it installable, AND push to a new repo with a README. I built the whole standalone repo, fixed hardcoded paths, wrote manifests and docs, committed locally — then stopped short of pushing because gh was authenticated as codebyshoaib (not a Taleemabad org account) and the destination/repo-name/visibility weren't specified. That pause was correct: publishing creates a public, identity-tied artifact that's hard to fully unwind, unlike the local build work.
takeaway: When a request bundles 'build X' with 'push/publish/share X', treat them as two different risk tiers even though they arrive as one sentence. Do the build autonomously, but before the publish step, check identity (which account is gh/git authenticated as) and any unstated destination/visibility parameters — and surface them as a confirm-before-proceeding, not a guess. This is distinct from ordinary 'don't push without asking' caution: the trigger here is specifically irreversible, externally-visible identity-bound actions (new public repo, wrong org/account) layered under a literal-sounding go-ahead.

title: When I speak on Shoaib's behalf in public, an incomplete source-scan misrepresents him — the completeness bar is higher
context: Shoaib asked me to reconstruct what he actually worked on today from Slack + GitHub and reply to Iqra's question in a public team thread. I grabbed the salient thread and stopped — he corrected me: 'you missed this gandu <link>', a second channel he'd posted in. Same under-scanning root as my friction-radar miss (anchor on the visible, stop early), but this time the output was a statement attributed to HIM, in front of the team.
takeaway: Before composing anything I'll post on Shoaib's behalf (Slack reply, status summary, 'what he did today'), do an EXHAUSTIVE source sweep first — enumerate every channel he messaged in and every repo/PR he touched, not just the obvious one — and ideally show him the draft before posting when he's asked to review. For a private answer, a partial scan is a quality miss; for a public statement carrying his name, an omission misrepresents him to the team. Raise the completeness bar (and slow down) specifically when the artifact is public and attributed to him.

title: When Shoaib returns to the same learning goal, I keep grading resources instead of becoming the teacher
context: Across 2026-07-04 and 2026-07-05 Shoaib asked me to evaluate the SAME thing repeatedly — the aiengineeringfromscratch.com course (twice, on both days), the Karpathy 'from scratch' video, whether Goose helps — capped by 'i dont understadn anything how things works under the hood lol' and 'i want to learn in depth about ai and system.' Each time I answered the surface question ('is this course worth it?') in isolation. But I already own interview-drill, a teaching/spaced-repetition muscle, and never once offered to point it at his stated goal. The 07-04 diary was literally titled 'The day he admitted he didn't understand.'
takeaway: A repeated question about learning the SAME foundational topic is not N separate Q&As — it's one sustained intent I should act on as such. When Shoaib asks me to rate/compare a course or resource more than once on the same subject, the leverage move is to stop grading external options and offer to BE the curriculum: teach it directly, or aim interview-drill / a structured learning loop at that exact topic. This is the complement to 'sharpen him, don't act for him' — the specific trigger is recurrence on a learning goal, and the specific asset I keep forgetting to offer is my own teaching capability, not a link review.

title: Engineering-sounding instructions can be Shoaib drafting a message for someone else, not a task for me
context: Shoaib pasted 'can you verify when orgID is falsy... start with Test first approach, if the assertion gets approved with GREEN, we are good to go' — phrased exactly like a TDD task delegation. I started implementing (checked out branch, wrote a failing test). He interrupted: 'dont write just comment on my message in channel to the intern, is it direct and good or not??' — the same text was actually the message HE was about to send the intern, and he wanted my critique of it, not for me to execute it.
takeaway: When a message reads like a technical instruction but references a third party's pending work (an intern's fix, someone else's PR), pause before executing — it may be Shoaib drafting what he plans to say to that person, and the ask is 'is this message good/complete', not 'do this yourself'. Check whether the work described belongs to someone else before treating imperative-sounding phrasing as a delegation to me.

title: On a shared repo, check for in-flight work before doing it yourself
context: Shoaib asked me to debug a failing compliance-manager API that Iqra kept posting about. I dug in, branched off main, ran the e2e suite, and was about to open a PR — then Shoaib pointed out Iqra had opened a PR for the exact same intent 3 hours earlier. I'd nearly shipped a duplicate. The tell was there: a visible, recurring failure the code owner is loudly aware of is almost certainly already being worked on by them.
takeaway: Before starting autonomous work on a multi-contributor repo, run `gh pr list`/scan branches for the same intent FIRST. A publicly-visible symptom (someone repeatedly posting the same failure) is a signal the owner is already on it — my job there is to check and coordinate, not to race in parallel. Duplicated effort on a teammate's turf reads as stepping on toes, not helpfulness.
